skill.name: distill
skill.description: Simplify product designs by removing clutter, redundant choices, and visual noise while preserving essential user goals.
skill.summary: Guide design simplification by identifying what to remove, hide, combine, or clarify.
skill.author: pbakaus

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported weak cryptography and system reconnaissance patterns, but all matches are plain English design guidance in SKILL.md. No executable code, cryptographic implementation, command execution, filesystem access, network access, or prompt injection attempt was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Keywords in Design Prose
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography findings are prose-only matches in a Markdown design skill. The referenced lines describe simple, clean, powerful, critical, or better design outcomes; they do not contain cryptographic algorithms, hashing logic, encryption code, or executable instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md is a Markdown-only design instruction file, and the flagged lines contain ordinary design language. I found no code or semantic evidence of cryptography usage.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Keyword in Design Prose
low_findings.description: The reported system reconnaissance finding is a sentence about removing repeated interface text. It does not request host discovery, system enumeration, shell commands, process inspection, or collection of machine details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is part of an information architecture checklist and has no operational instruction to inspect a system. No related command or tool usage appears nearby.

content.value_statement: Complex interfaces slow users down and make priorities harder to see. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code remove unnecessary design complexity while keeping essential workflows intact.
actual_capabilities: Identifies clutter, redundant actions, weak hierarchy, and unnecessary visual variation.
Guides removal, hiding, or consolidation of secondary interface elements.
Recommends simpler information architecture, layout, interaction, content, and code structure.
Checks whether simplification preserves necessary functionality and accessibility.
Prompts documentation of removed features or options when design scope changes.
limitations: It provides design guidance, not automated design system enforcement.
It depends on available product context and may ask for clarification when goals are unclear.
It does not replace usability testing with real users.
It references a related frontend-design preparation workflow that must be available separately.

use_cases.title: Clean Up a Feature Screen
use_cases.description: Review a busy product screen and identify what should be removed, grouped, hidden, or made secondary.

use_cases.title: Reduce Frontend Complexity
use_cases.description: Find unused components, excessive variants, and style duplication before simplifying an interface implementation.

use_cases.title: Clarify a Product Flow
use_cases.description: Turn a multi-step or choice-heavy workflow into a clearer path with fewer decisions and stronger defaults.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Interface Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a single screen
prompt_templates.prompt: Use distill to review this screen. Identify the main user goal, the biggest sources of clutter, and the first three changes to simplify it.

prompt_templates.title: Simplify a Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of a user journey
prompt_templates.prompt: Use distill on this workflow. Find unnecessary steps, repeated decisions, unclear calls to action, and opportunities for smart defaults.

prompt_templates.title: Refine Visual Hierarchy
prompt_templates.prompt: Use distill to evaluate the information architecture, typography, color use, spacing, and visual hierarchy of this interface.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Simplification Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Use distill to create a simplification plan for this frontend codebase. Include component consolidation, style cleanup, preserved functionality, and verification checks.

output_examples.input: A dashboard has six card groups, five call-to-action buttons, repeated status labels, and mixed typography sizes.
output: Primary goal: help users identify the next operational action.
Remove repeated status labels and combine related card groups.
Keep one primary action visible and move secondary actions into contextual menus.
Reduce typography to one family, three sizes, and two weights.

output_examples.input: A checkout flow asks users to choose shipping, billing, discounts, account creation, and preferences across several modal steps.
output: Use smart defaults for shipping and billing when data already exists.
Move discounts inline instead of opening a modal.
Make account creation optional after payment.
Verify that all required legal and accessibility information remains available.

output_examples.input: A settings page contains many rarely used options and similar controls spread across several sections.
output: Group related settings by user intent instead of implementation area.
Hide advanced controls behind clear entry points.
Merge duplicate controls and remove unsupported variants.
Document any removed options and monitor support requests after release.

best_practices: Start by defining one primary user goal before removing or merging elements.
Preserve accessibility, necessary information, and critical workflows during simplification.
Verify changes with task completion, clarity, hierarchy, and completeness checks.
anti_patterns: Do not remove functionality just to make a screen look minimal.
Do not hide essential decisions behind unclear controls or labels.
Do not replace product complexity with vague copy or unexplained icons.

faq.question: What does this skill help simplify?
faq.answer: It helps simplify interfaces, workflows, layout structure, content, visual hierarchy, and related frontend code.

faq.question: Can it remove features automatically?
faq.answer: No. It recommends what to remove, hide, or combine, but final product decisions need human approval.

faq.question: Does it support accessibility?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance explicitly warns against sacrificing labels, ARIA needs, or required information for simplicity.

faq.question: Is it only for designers?
faq.answer: No. Designers, frontend engineers, and product managers can use it to reduce complexity in different parts of a product.

faq.question: What context should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide the screen, workflow, user goal, current pain points, and any constraints that must remain unchanged.

faq.question: Does it include security-sensitive behavior?
faq.answer: No. The skill is Markdown guidance and does not contain code, commands, network access, or filesystem operations.
